text: In this video we present a patient with a large incisional hernia after an emergency laparotomy. He will undergo hernia repair with laparoscopic component separation.

The surgeon marks the hernia and key surgical landmarks on the abdomen. The external oblique aponeurosis is dissected to expose the internal oblique muscle. An avascular intermuscular plane is developed, and air seal trocars are inserted. The external oblique aponeurosis is transected, allowing the rectus abdominis muscles to medialize.

The surgeon digitally develops the intermuscular plane over the ribs, facilitating muscular transection. A dilating balloon trocar is placed to expand the plane. The external oblique aponeurosis is incised laterally to the semilunar line and transected downwards to the inguinal ligament, avoiding a full thickness abdominal wall defect. Scarpus fascia is also transected for maximum mobilization. The air seal maintains stable pneumo and efficient smoke extraction.

The surgeon places a five-millimeter port cranial to the inguinal ligament after sufficiently developing the plane. Using a ligature, the external oblique muscle is transected, with further development of the precostal plane preceding the transection. The right-sided component separation is briefly demonstrated.

After completing the component separation, the surgeon achieves pneumoperitoneum and performs adhesiolysis to free the abdomen of adhesions. The hernia is approximated using mattress sutures. An intra-abdominal Symbiotex mesh, measuring twenty by thirty centimeters, is placed and attached to the ventral abdominal wall using Absorbatex.
